summary: Gruber reviews the iPhone 11, 11 Pro, and 11 Pro Max, arguing that the camera system is overwhelmingly the defining feature of these phones — to the point where 'iCamera' would be a more apt name. He finds the year-over-year camera improvements impressive for the second consecutive year, with Night Mode being a standout addition. He recommends the 128 GB iPhone 11 as the best value for most people, notes that removing 3D Touch likely enabled bigger batteries and significantly better battery life in the Pro models, and concludes that Apple is effectively building a real standalone camera right in our pockets.
key_points: The iPhone has evolved so thoroughly into a camera that Apple spent virtually all stage time on camera features and none on phone calls — a complete inversion from the original 2007 iPhone introduction.
The regular iPhone 11 at $699 (down $50 from last year's XR) is the best phone for most people, with the 128 GB model at $750 being the sweet spot of the lineup.

Removing 3D Touch in favor of Haptic Touch freed internal space for larger batteries, explaining the dramatic 4-5 hour battery life improvement in the Pro models despite a slight regression in interaction responsiveness.
Night Mode produces remarkable results in extreme low light, effectively letting you take handheld multi-second exposures that would be impossible in traditional photography.
iOS 13.0 shipped in a rough state that feels like a late-stage beta, with 13.1 needed urgently for stability.
money_quotes: Driven by the iPhone, the word phone has simply taken on a new definition — a pocket-sized touchscreen personal computer with wireless networking and one or more cameras.
Try convincing a six-year-old that a landline telephone is a 'phone' — they won't believe you.
They don't have to rename it 'iCamera' — these phones scream this is a camera just by looking at them.

Imagine if only some iOS devices had touchscreens. If that were the case we'd see half-hearted, inconsistent support for multitouch across the system and in third-party apps. That's what happened to 3D Touch.
Night Mode doesn't require anything more than casually steady hands. It's like being able to hold your hands as steady as a tripod.

Several times over the past few years, I've had conversations along the lines of, 'I know they're never going to do this, but wouldn't it be cool if Apple made a real camera?' As the iPhone camera system evolves, I'm starting to think Apple is making a real camera, right under our noses — or perhaps better said, right in our pockets.
13.0 feels like a late stage beta.
The iPhone 11 Camera app is very different, but I took to it right away. It feels less like an app and more like a camera.
key_insight: The iPhone has so completely transformed from a phone into a camera that Apple's primary engineering and marketing story is now computational photography, and the iPhone 11 generation represents Apple genuinely building a serious camera that happens to also make phone calls.
